title: 10 best herbal teas for taste changes
intro_desc: Herbal teas for taste changes are a natural solution to combat altered taste perceptions, often resulting from medication, age, or underlying health conditions. These teas contain concentrated plant extracts that stimulate and balance the taste buds, restoring normal flavor perception. Examples of such herbal teas include ginger, peppermint, and licorice root, which have been shown to improve appetite and enjoyability of meals in individuals with taste changes. This can significantly enhance overall quality of life by allowing people to savor their favorite foods and drinks again.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint teas helps with taste changes because of its natural ability to stimulate saliva production and refresh the palate. The menthol content in peppermint tea acts as a gentle cleanser, removing any lingering flavors or odors that may be affecting one's sense of taste. Additionally, peppermint's calming properties can help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, allowing the taste buds to function optimally. As a result, sipping on herbal peppermint tea can leave one with a refreshed and revitalized sense of taste.
remedy_properties: Digestive relief: Peppermint tea's menthol content can relax digestive muscles and reduce inflammation, helping to alleviate symptoms like nausea and bloating associated with taste changes.

Antioxidant activity: Peppermint tea contains antioxidants that neutralize free radicals, which can damage taste buds and affect the sense of taste, helping to preserve and restore normal taste function.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids present in peppermint tea have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in the mouth, throat, and digestive tract, making it easier to experience flavors and textures normally.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds, peppermint roots are valued for their calming and digestive benefits, which can help alleviate bitter or unpleasant tastes.
Rhyzomes: The underground stem of the peppermint plant, rhyzomes contain unique flavor compounds that can enhance the taste of teas and promote a sense of clarity.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves per cup of boiling water.
Heat the measured water in a teapot or kettle until it reaches a rolling boil.
Pour the boiling water over the mint leaves and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or piece of cheesecloth to remove solids.
Add honey or lemon to taste if desired, and enjoy within 30 minutes of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with taste changes because it calms the nervous system, reducing anxiety and stress that can alter one's sense of taste. The herb's soothing properties also ease digestive issues, such as nausea or bloating, which can affect flavor perception. Additionally, lemon balm's subtle citrus flavor can stimulate the appetite and increase salivation, further enhancing the enjoyment of food and drinks. As a result, sipping on herbal lemon balm tea can help restore balance to the senses and appreciate flavors once again.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Lemon balm tea's high antioxidant content helps protect the tongue and mouth from free radical damage, which can contribute to taste changes caused by oxidative stress.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in lemon balm tea can reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, alleviating symptoms of taste changes such as dry mouth, burning sensations, or a metallic taste.
Soothing and calming effects: Lemon balm tea's calming properties can help reduce stress and anxiety, which are common triggers for taste changes such as aversion to certain foods or flavors.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bitter compounds, lemon balm roots can add a pleasant bitterness to tea, balancing out sweet flavors and promoting digestion.
Seeds: With their crunchy texture and nutty flavor, lemon balm seeds can add a unique dimension to tea, making it more interesting and engaging.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of dried melissa officinalis leaves or flowers for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the herb mixture in boiling water for seven to ten minutes to release its flavors and oils.
Strain the tea using a piece of cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove any loose particles.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving, which helps bring out its subtle flavor characteristics.
Store any leftover tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to three days to preserve its potency and freshness.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginger teas helps with taste changes because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which soothe the digestive system and alleviate symptoms of taste bud fatigue. The warm, spicy flavor of ginger also stimulates saliva production, which can help to refresh the palate and restore a sense of taste. Additionally, ginger's natural antibacterial properties may help to eliminate any lingering bacteria that could be affecting one's sense of smell and taste. This results in a clearer, more refined sense of taste, making it easier to enjoy a variety of flavors once again.
remedy_properties: Digestive enzymes: Ginger's digestive enzymes, such as zingibain, help to break down proteins and reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can alleviate nausea and loss of appetite associated with chemotherapy or radiation treatment.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: Ginger contains anti-inflammatory compounds like gingerol and shogaol that have been shown to reduce inflammation and pain in the mouth and throat, making it easier to eat and drink after a taste change diagnosis.
Antioxidants: Ginger's antioxidants help to protect the body against free radicals that can damage healthy cells and tissues, which can contribute to changes in taste perception.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds, roots are often used to create teas that stimulate digestion and alleviate nausea.
Rhyzomes: The thick, underground stem of the ginger plant, rhyzomes have a pungent flavor and aroma, making them ideal for teas that help with nausea and inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried ginger root and bring it to room temperature.
Boil one cup of water in a medium-sized pot for five minutes.
Add the dried ginger root to the boiling water and steep for seven minutes.
Strain the tea mixture into a large cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Allow the tea to cool to drinking temperature and consume immediately.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Roselle teas helps with taste changes because it contains a unique blend of antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals that can help to stimulate the sense of taste. The tea's sweet and slightly tart flavor profile also helps to balance out the bitterness often associated with taste changes. Additionally, roselle teas' ability to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat can help to alleviate symptoms of taste disorders such as dry mouth, soreness, and numbness, allowing for a more normal sense of taste to return.
remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Roselle tea contains high levels of antioxidants, which can help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the body, potentially alleviating unpleasant taste changes associated with certain medical conditions.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: The anti-inflammatory properties of Roselle tea may help alleviate symptoms such as dry mouth, burning sensations, or altered taste perceptions commonly experienced during chemotherapy or other medical treatments.

Digestive benefits: Roselle tea has been traditionally used to soothe digestive issues like indigestion and bloating, which can also contribute to changes in taste perception; its potential anti-inflammatory effects on the gut may also help restore normal gut function.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in saponins, which give the tea a sweet and slightly bitter flavor, helping to balance out bitter or unpleasant tastes.
Rhyzomes: Contain compounds that provide a warm and comforting sensation in the mouth, making it ideal for soothing sore throats or calming digestive issues.
Leaves: With their high concentration of antioxidants and flavonoids, they add a subtle bitterness and earthy flavor to the tea, perfect for balancing out sweet or fruity notes.
remedy_recipe: Rinse one teaspoon of dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers with boiling water.
Steep the rinsed flowers in one cup of boiling water for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Discard the solids and add honey to taste if desired.
Serve the tea warm or at room temperature within 30 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chamomile teas helps with taste changes because it calms the digestive system, allowing the body to better process and absorb flavors. The anti-inflammatory properties of chamomile soothe the mucous membranes in the mouth and throat, reducing irritation and discomfort associated with altered tastes. Additionally, chamomile's natural calming effects can reduce stress and anxiety, which are common triggers for taste disturbances. By promoting digestive harmony and relaxation, chamomile teas provide a gentle and soothing solution to alleviate changes in taste.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Chamomile's anti-inflammatory compounds can soothe and calm the digestive system, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with taste changes caused by mouth sores, radiation therapy, or chemotherapy.

Antioxidant properties: Chamomile's antioxidants help protect the mouth and throat from oxidative stress, which can damage taste buds and reduce flavor perception, making it easier to enjoy food and drinks again.

Sedative and calming effects: Chamomile's natural sedatives can promote relaxation and calmness, reducing anxiety and stress related to changes in taste, smell, or appetite that often occur with illness or treatment.
remedy_parts: Roots: Known for their soothing properties, chamomile roots can help reduce bitterness in tea blends and create a calming effect.
Flowers: The delicate flowers of chamomile have a sweet, apple-like flavor that can enhance the overall taste of herbal teas.
remedy_recipe: Measure accurately one teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Heat filtered or bottled water in a saucepan to a boil and let it simmer for at least five minutes.
Pour the boiling hot water over the measured chamomille flowers in a teapot or infuser.
Allow the tea mixture to steep for seven to ten minutes, depending on desired strength and flavor.
Strain the tea into a cup using a strainer or tea infuser, then add honey or lemon to taste as desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Kansas coneflower teas helps with taste changes because it contains Echinacea, a natural herb that has been traditionally used to support immune function. The antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds present in the tea may help to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can cause unpleasant tastes or odors. Additionally, the tea's soothing properties may calm any irritation or discomfort associated with taste changes, allowing for a more balanced and pleasant sensation when eating or drinking.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: The anti-inflammatory properties of Kansas Coneflower tea may alleviate inflammation in the mouth and throat that can cause changes in taste, such as bitter or metallic flavors.
Immune system support: By supporting the immune system, Kansas Coneflower tea may help prevent infections that can disrupt taste buds and lead to unusual tastes, such as sweet or sour sensations.

Barks: The barks of the Kansas coneflower have a unique, slightly sweet and woody flavor that complements sweet or sour flavors, making it an ideal choice for masking taste imbalances.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of dried echinacea angustifolia flowers per 8 ounces of boiling water.
Add the measured echinacea angustifolia to a teapot or infuser and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or fine-mesh sieve to remove the herbal particles.
Add one tablespoon of honey if desired to balance out the bitter flavor of the echinacea angustifolia.
Let the tea cool down slightly before serving it in a cup and enjoying the calming effects.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sweet briar teas helps with taste changes because it stimulates saliva production, which helps to regenerate and repair taste buds. remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh rosa rubiginosa flowers or dried petals for 2 ounces of loose-leaf tea or 4 tea bags.
Combine harvested flowers with boiling water in a large teapot or infuser and steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or carafe to remove excess solids.
Add honey or lemon to taste if desired and stir well to combine after steeping for 5-7 minutes more.
Serve warm or chilled depending on personal preference and enjoy within 24 hours of preparation for optimal flavor and aroma.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English lavender teas helps with taste changes because its unique botanical properties soothe and calm the senses, reducing irritation in the mouth and throat. The tea's subtle floral flavor also gently masks any unpleasant or bitter notes, allowing more nuanced flavors to emerge. Digestive soothing properties: Lavender tea's digestive benefits can alleviate stomach upset, nausea, or acid reflux associated with changes in taste, promoting a sense of comfort and ease while consuming the tea.

Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects: English Lavender's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat caused by taste changes, soothing discomfort and promoting healthy tissue.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried lavandula angustifolia flowers according to your local regulations for an optimal amount of one ounce per quart water ratio.
Rinse the harvested herbs gently with cold running water to remove any dirt debris or impurities from the buds.
Steep one teaspoon of dried or two tablespoons of fresh lavandula angustifolia flowers in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea leaves and discard the solids carefully to prevent any remaining bitterness affecting the final taste.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving hot or refrigerating for later consumption within a 24-hour timeframe for maximum flavor and aroma preservation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme teas helps with taste changes because it has a calming effect on the palate, soothing any inflammation or irritation that may be affecting one's sense of taste. The tea's subtle flavor profile also gently stimulates the taste buds, helping to revive and restore their function. Additionally, thyme's natural antioxidants and compounds have been shown to help reduce oxidative stress and promote overall digestive health, which can further enhance the body's ability to process and perceive flavors.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Thyme's antimicrobial compounds, such as thymol, help combat harmful bacteria and fungi in the mouth, which can alter taste perception and cause bad breath.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Thyme's anti-inflammatory compounds, like rosmarinic acid, reduce inflammation in the oral cavity, which can alleviate symptoms of dry mouth, bad taste, and mouth sores that are common with taste changes.

Expectorant properties: Thyme's expectorant properties help loosen and clear mucus from the respiratory tract, including the sinuses and lungs, which can improve taste perception by reducing congestion and sinus pressure.
remedy_parts: Roots: Thyme roots have a rich, earthy flavor and aroma that can help mask bitter or unpleasant tastes in medicinal teas.
Flowers: Thyme flowers add a delicate, floral note to teas that can enhance their overall flavor and aroma.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality fresh or dried thymus vulgaris leaves and flowers in sufficient quantities for your desired dosage.
Combine one teaspoon of dried thymus vulgaris herb or two teaspoons of fresh thymus vulgaris herb with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the herbal mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the bioactive compounds to infuse into the hot water.
Strain the tea solution through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup, discarding the solids and retaining the liquid extract.
Allow the tea to cool down to a comfortable drinking temperature before consuming the 1-2 cups of herbal brew daily for optimal results within 30 minutes after meal times.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sage teas helps with taste changes because it has been shown to enhance flavor perception, particularly in individuals who have experienced a decline in their sense of taste due to age, illness, or certain medications. The antioxidants and volatile oils present in sage tea work together to stimulate the taste buds and improve the overall sensation of flavors. remedy_properties: Antioxidant Properties: Sage tea's antioxidants help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body, which can contribute to altered tastes and smells by neutralizing free radicals that can damage taste buds.

Anti-Inflammatory Properties: Sage tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as rosmarinic acid, help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can cause changes in taste perception by reducing swelling and irritation of the mucous membranes.

Gastrointestinal Soothing Properties: Sage tea's anti-spasmodic and carminative properties can help soothe and calm digestive issues like bloating, cramps, and nausea, which are common causes of altered tastes and smells by improving gut health and reducing discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are used because they have a rich, earthy flavor that can add depth and warmth to teas.
Barks: The barks are used because they contain tannins that can help balance and harmonize bitter flavors in teas.
remedy_recipe: Gather dried salvia officinalis leaves and measure out 1-2 teaspoons for each cup of boiling water.
Heat 8 ounces of water in a teapot or kettle until it reaches a rolling boil.
Steep the measured salvia officinalis leaves in the hot water for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to infuse.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup and discard the solids.
Allow the tea to cool to a comfortable drinking temperature and enjoy within 30 minutes of preparation.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat taste changes with herbal teas is to consume a variety of brews to stimulate your taste buds. Ginger tea, peppermint tea, and lemon balm tea are excellent options as they have natural digestive properties that can help restore flavor sensitivity. Additionally, drink small amounts frequently throughout the day to ensure optimal flavor and aroma experience.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to taste changes that are treated with herbal teas are digestive issues such as bloating, constipation, and indigestion. Herbal teas like peppermint, ginger, and chamomile help soothe the stomach and alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). Additionally, herbal teas like echinacea, slippery elm, and licorice root can treat throat infections, sore throats, and coughs by reducing inflammation and promoting healing.
